retrieved_docs.title: Epidural (Archived) -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Neuraxial anesthesia or analgesia is the injection of the local anesthetic in the areas surrounding nerve roots from the central nervous system (CNS). Spinal anesthesia involves the injection of the local anesthetic in the cerebrospinal fluid (CSF) flowing in the subarachnoid space while epidural anesthesia or analgesia the local anesthetic (LA) is infiltrated in the fat tissue that surrounds the spinal nerve roots in the epidural space using a catheter inserted in the space through an epidural needle.

retrieved_docs.title: Epidural Hematoma -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Identify key clinical signs and symptoms associated with epidural hematoma, including headache, vomiting, altered consciousness, focal neurologic deficits, and the classic lucid interval, to initiate timely evaluation. Differentiate epidural hematoma from subdural hematoma and other types of intracranial hemorrhage by analyzing characteristic findings on noncontrast head computed tomography, such as the lens-shaped (biconvex) appearance of epidural hematoma confined by suture lines. Assess changes in neurologic status and imaging findings in patients with epidural hematoma to guide intervention and prevent complications. Collaborate with interprofessional healthcare team members to optimize communication, triage, and coordinated care for patients with epidural hematoma. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.content: Notably, DAI should be strongly considered in patients who fail to improve after receiving surgical evacuation of subdural or epidural hematomas. Conversely, if patients drastically improve after surgical evacuation of a subdural or epidural hematoma, DAI may not be present.

retrieved_docs.title: Thoracic Segmental Spinal Anesthesia -- Anatomy and Physiology
retrieved_docs.content: The anatomy of the thoracic and lumbar regions of the spine is similar but has some distinct differences that are important for giving anesthetic in the thoracic spine. Anatomical studies have been performed using MRI to better define the space within the spine for regional anesthesia. These MRI images have shown that the mid to lower thoracic segment of the cord lies anteriorly, where there is a CSF-filled space between the dura and the cord. In contrast, the spinal cord and the cauda equina are touching the dura mater posteriorly in the lumbar region. This has demonstrated there is a greater depth of the posterior subarachnoid space in the thoracic spinal cord. In one MRI imaging study with 50 patients, the space between the dura mater and spinal cord in the thoracic spine measured at 7.75 mm at T5 and 5.88 mm at T10. [4] Intrathecal injections at mid-thoracic levels may have a minimum safe distance before the spinal needle contacts the spinal cord tissue.

retrieved_docs.title: Epidural Hematoma -- Treatment / Management -- Nonoperative Management
retrieved_docs.content: According to established guidelines, a nonoperative approach may be appropriate for patients with acute EDH who meet all of the following criteria: EDH volume less than 30 mL Clot thickness less than 15 mm Midline shift less than 5 mm GCS score greater than 8 Absence of focal neurologic deficits

retrieved_docs.title: Epidural Anesthesia -- Equipment -- Ultrasound-Guided Epidural Anesthesia
retrieved_docs.content: Clinicians may choose to place an epidural catheter with ultrasound guidance. Ultrasound technology allows real-time visualization of the relevant anatomical structures, potentially improving the accuracy and safety of epidural catheter placement. The advantages of using ultrasound guidance for epidural anesthesia include better identification of the epidural space, fewer needle passes and attempts, and a lower incidence of complications such as unintentional dural puncture, infection, and hematoma formation. However, further research is needed to establish the superiority of ultrasound-guided epidural anesthesia over traditional landmark-based techniques. [22]

retrieved_docs.content: Intravenous fluids: Fluids are typically administered before an epidural is performed to prevent hypotensive episodes.

retrieved_docs.title: Epidural Anesthesia -- Anatomy and Physiology -- The Spinal Cord and Epidural Space
retrieved_docs.content: The adult spinal cord is approximately 45 cm shorter than the spinal canal; the cord typically ends at the L1 vertebra in 50% of adults and extends to the L2 vertebra in about 40%. Recent studies have controverted previously held notions regarding the termination of the spinal cord in the newborn; current evidence indicates that the conus medullaris of the neonate also terminates at the L2 vertebra. Below this anatomical level, the lumbar and sacral nerves converge to form the cauda equina. The spinal cord is suspended in cerebrospinal fluid and surrounded by the arachnoid mater. The arachnoid mater and subarachnoid space extend caudally to S2 in adults, S3 in children, and S4 in newborns. The arachnoid mater closely approximates the dura mater, which is attached to the spine by its outer endosteal portion. The arachnoid mater envelopes the brain intracranially and the spinal cord and extends through the intervertebral foramina to the epineural connective tissues of the spinal nerves. [2]

retrieved_docs.title: Regional Anesthetic Blocks -- Technique or Treatment -- Neuraxial Anesthesia (Spinal/Epidural/Combined)
retrieved_docs.content: Neuraxial blocks consist of placing a needle through the back to inject a specific drug into the subarachnoid space for spinal anesthesia or in the epidural space for epidural anesthesia. Both techniques can be applied together. [4] Epidural Anesthesia

retrieved_docs.title: Epidural Hematoma -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: Angiographic evaluation in 35 surgical cases identified meningeal arterial extravasation with epidural arteriovenous shunting in 60% of patients. Individuals with shunting experienced frequent clinical deterioration, suggesting that ongoing hemorrhage contributes to hematoma expansion and herniation risk. [13] Shunting may also increase the likelihood of a lucid interval. By diverting blood flow, the arteriovenous shunt can delay hematoma formation and the rise in intracranial pressure (ICP), postponing symptom onset. [14]

retrieved_docs.title: Epidural Anesthesia -- Contraindications -- Absolute Contraindications
retrieved_docs.content: Epidural anesthesia is contraindicated if any of the following factors are present: Patient refusal Local infection at the puncture site Increased intracranial pressure Traumatic spinal cord injury [14]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 4 -- Community 2894
retrieved_docs.content: Cerebral edema (تكثر الدبق الدماغي) may be a finding of disease that presents with headache (صداع), indicating that increased intracranial pressure or brain swelling can manifest as head pain. In addition, lumbar puncture (البزل القطني (LP)) is clinically associated with headache, reflecting the common post‑procedural symptom that patients may experience after cerebrospinal fluid sampling. Together, these connections underscore that headache can arise both from underlying intracranial pathology such as cerebral edema and from iatrogenic causes like lumbar puncture, highlighting the need for careful assessment of headache etiology in clinical practice.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 3 -- Community 2154
retrieved_docs.content: Abnormal and nonspecific findings in the cerebrospinal fluid (CSF) are clinically associated with headache, indicating that patients who exhibit such CSF abnormalities often present with this symptom. Likewise, lumbar puncture (LP) is also clinically associated with headache, reflecting the common occurrence of post‑procedural headache following the invasive sampling of CSF. In the broader diagnostic framework, “other screening for suspected conditions” includes abnormal and nonspecific CSF findings as a subcategory, linking this screening approach to the detection of CSF abnormalities that may underlie headache. Together, these relationships highlight the importance of recognizing headache as a key clinical manifestation linked both to intrinsic CSF pathology and to the lumbar puncture procedure itself, and they underscore the role of comprehensive screening strategies in identifying CSF abnormalities that may guide patient evaluation and management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 4 -- Community 2897
retrieved_docs.content: The central nervous system (centrální nervový systém) is described as a physical component of the dura mater, indicating its anatomical relationship within the meningeal coverings. In clinical assessment, the central nervous system serves as the finding site for central nervous system injury (إصابة الجهاز العصبي المركزي), highlighting its relevance as the location where such pathological findings are identified. Understanding that the central nervous system is both anatomically linked to the dura mater and is the primary site of CNS injuries informs diagnostic and therapeutic approaches in neurology and neurosurgery.

Neurological trauma is illustrated by “Subarachnoid hemorrhage following injury with open intracranial wound, unspecified state of consciousness”, which is associated with internal injury (إصابة داخلية) and, like sports, arm, and back injuries, belongs to the SMQ hierarchy. Overall, the taxonomy integrates transport and non‑transport mechanisms, anatomical injury locations, intentional versus unintentional intent, and temporal/contextual factors, providing a comprehensive framework for classifying, querying, and managing the full spectrum of traumatic presentations in clinical practice.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 3 -- Community 2678
retrieved_docs.content: Injury of central nervous system due to birth trauma is a condition in which the pathological finding is located within the central nervous system. The central nervous system is anatomically linked to the dura mater, as the dura mater constitutes a physical component of the protective meningeal layers that surround the brain and spinal cord. Consequently, injuries to the central nervous system, such as those caused by birth trauma, involve structures that are encased by the dura mater, underscoring the importance of the dura mater in shielding the central nervous system and influencing clinical assessment and management of such injuries.
